title: Most RAG Systems Fail at Retrieval, Not the Model
linkedin_md:
# Most RAG Systems Fail at Retrieval, Not the Model

When a RAG system gives a bad answer, the first instinct is usually to blame the model.

The model hallucinated. The model ignored context. The model is not good enough. We need a bigger one. We need a better prompting framework. We need reranking, agents, graphs, long context, or some new retrieval architecture.

Sometimes that is true.

Most of the time, the system simply did not retrieve the right evidence.

The model cannot synthesize facts it never sees. It cannot reason over a document that was chunked badly, filtered out, buried at rank 17, or stripped of the metadata needed to interpret it. It cannot answer a question about version-specific behavior if retrieval mixes old docs, new docs, support tickets, Slack exports, and stale wiki pages into one undifferentiated pile.

RAG quality usually breaks before generation starts.

I hit this with my own agent system. It kept citing a stale doc, confidently. The real problem was not the model. I had dozens of overlapping docs scattered across the workspace, multiple versions of the same fact, and retrieval happily served whichever chunk scored highest that day.

The fix had nothing to do with prompts or a bigger model. I compiled the scattered docs into one canonical wiki, Karpathy style: each fact lives in one place, regenerated when sources change. Retrieval got boring and correct overnight.

Same model. Same embeddings. The only thing that changed was what it was allowed to see.

## The failure mode is usually boring

Most broken RAG systems have some version of these problems:

The chunks are the wrong shape.

The embedding query does not match how users ask questions.

Metadata exists but is not used.

Old content competes with current content.

Evaluation only checks final answers, not retrieved context.

Retrieval returns plausible documents instead of necessary documents.

These are not glamorous problems. They do not look like research. They look like data plumbing, indexing discipline, and evaluation hygiene.

But this is where the leverage is.

A model with mediocre retrieval will produce confident nonsense. A smaller model with clean retrieval can often produce useful answers because it is grounded in the right evidence.

## Chunking is a product decision

Chunking is not just a preprocessing step. It defines what the model is allowed to know at inference time.

A 200-token chunk might be great for precise lookup, but terrible if the answer needs surrounding constraints. A 2,000-token chunk might preserve context, but dilute the embedding and retrieve half-relevant blobs.

A practical starting point:

Use 300 to 800 token chunks for documentation-like content.

Preserve headings, section titles, file paths, and document titles inside the chunk text.

Use 10 to 20 percent overlap only when content is continuous prose.

Avoid blind fixed-window chunking for structured docs, API references, legal text, tables, or tickets.

Chunk by semantic boundaries where possible: section, function, issue, policy, decision, or FAQ entry.

The goal is not beautiful chunks. The goal is chunks that answer real user questions without requiring the model to reconstruct missing context.

If a chunk needs the previous three chunks to make sense, it is probably not a good retrieval unit.

## Metadata beats cleverness

A lot of teams underuse metadata because vector search feels more elegant.

That is a mistake.

If your corpus has fields like product area, doc type, version, language, owner, created date, updated date, permissions, region, or customer segment, those are not optional decorations. They are retrieval controls.

Filtering by metadata before vector search often beats retrieving globally and hoping the embedding model figures it out.

Examples:

If a user asks about Python SDK behavior, filter to Python docs.

If they ask about the current policy, filter out archived pages.

If they ask about a specific account, enforce permissions before retrieval.

If they ask about version 2.1, do not let version 1.8 compete unless fallback is intentional.

This is boring, but it removes entire classes of failure.

Semantic search is good at fuzzy relevance. It is not a substitute for knowing which documents are allowed, current, or applicable.

## Hybrid retrieval is usually worth it

Dense embeddings are useful, but they are not magic.

They can miss exact identifiers, error codes, CLI flags, class names, ticket IDs, and rare terms. Keyword search is still very good at those.

For production RAG, hybrid retrieval is often a strong default:

Use BM25 or keyword search for exact lexical matches.

Use vector search for semantic matches.

Merge results.

Deduplicate.

Rerank the top 20 to 100 candidates.

Send the best 4 to 12 chunks to the model, depending on context budget and answer complexity.

The exact numbers depend on latency and corpus size, but the pattern is stable. Retrieve broadly enough to avoid missing the answer, then rerank tightly enough to avoid flooding the model.

A common mistake is retrieving top 5 directly from vector search and calling it done.

That is rarely enough.

Text-to-SQL taught me this the hard way. When a question spans multiple tables, plain vector search over schema descriptions falls apart. The right table is often not the most semantically similar one, and the tables you need for joins may never match the query text at all.

What worked was a fusion setup: vector search to get candidate tables, an LLM pass over the filtered schema info, and a fusion score to pick the top three tables before writing any SQL. More moving parts, but the generated SQL went from coin flip to dependable.

Top-k similarity was the baseline, not the answer.

## Evaluate retrieval separately

If you only evaluate final answers, you will misdiagnose failures.

For every test question, log:

The user query.

The rewritten retrieval query, if any.

The top retrieved chunks.

The chunk ranks.

The final context sent to the model.

The answer.

The citation or source used.

Then label retrieval directly.

Did the answerable evidence appear in the top 3?

Top 5?

Top 10?

Was it absent?

Was it present but lower than irrelevant chunks?

Was the retrieved chunk technically relevant but missing the key sentence?

This gives you a much clearer debugging loop.

If the gold evidence is not in the top 10, prompting will not save you. If the gold evidence is rank 8 but only top 4 are passed to the model, tune ranking or context budget. If the evidence is present and the model still fails, then you have a generation problem.

Do not mix these together.

## Query rewriting helps, but keep it constrained

LLMs can improve retrieval queries, especially when users ask vague or conversational questions.

But query rewriting can also destroy specificity.

Bad rewrite:

"Why is my build failing with ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND in the worker package?"

Becomes:

"Troubleshooting JavaScript build errors"

Now the exact error and package disappeared.

A better pattern is to generate multiple retrieval queries:

One exact query preserving identifiers.

One semantic paraphrase.

One keyword-style query with entities, versions, and error codes.

Then retrieve across all of them.

The rule is simple: never let a rewrite remove rare tokens from the original question. Rare tokens are often the most important part.

## Freshness matters more than people expect

Many RAG systems quietly rot because the index is stale.

Docs get updated. Policies change. APIs deprecate. Teams rename concepts. Old issues remain searchable forever.

You need a freshness policy.

For some corpora, hourly indexing matters. For others, daily is fine. For static docs, deployment-time indexing may be enough.

But the system should know:

When was this chunk indexed?

When was the source last updated?

Is this document active, deprecated, archived, or draft?

Should older versions be searchable by default?

If the model cites stale information, users will blame the AI. The actual bug is often that retrieval had no concept of time.

## The boring fixes

If your RAG system is unreliable, start here before adding complexity:

Inspect the top 10 retrieved chunks for 50 real user queries.

Add metadata filters for obvious constraints.

Fix chunk boundaries around headings and logical units.

Preserve titles, paths, and section hierarchy in chunk text.

Add hybrid keyword plus vector retrieval.

Rerank more candidates than you send to the model.

Create a small retrieval eval set with known supporting evidence.

Track whether gold evidence appears in top 3, top 5, and top 10.

Remove or downrank stale, duplicate, and low-authority content.

Log retrieval traces for every bad answer.

None of this is flashy.

But this is the work that moves RAG from demo to production.

## The takeaway

The model is the visible part of the system, so it gets the blame.

But in production RAG, retrieval is usually where the answer is won or lost.

Better prompts can make outputs cleaner. Bigger models can reason better. Agents can orchestrate more steps.

But if the right context never reaches the model, none of that matters.

The highest ROI fixes are usually unglamorous: better chunks, better filters, better freshness, better hybrid retrieval, and retrieval evals that show exactly what the model saw.

Start there.

---
**Tools I actually use**

- [Cursor](https://cursor.com) : where most of my agent-loop iteration happens
- [Beehiiv](https://www.beehiiv.com) : runs this newsletter's email list
- [Hostinger VPS](https://www.hostinger.com/vps-hosting) : the $12/mo box my agents live on
